sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: After 7 clicks: 9 charcoal pixels, 55 pink remaining. White areas changed shape when clicked. I've clicked charcoal, yellow, blue, black, and white objects. Haven't clicked dark gray yet.
- Plan: Try clicking on the dark gray area to see if it does something different or completes the level.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 8 actions; a strong score needs about 7 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check what happened after clicking on the dark gray area. I need to understand if this click did something different from the others.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after clicking dark gray
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('P', 'c', 'G'):
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']}")

ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"\nTop row: {ascii_lines[0]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
id=0 color=P pixels=54
  id=1 color=c pixels=10
  id=2 color=G pixels=2600
  id=10 color=c pixels=16

Top row: PPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPcccccccccc

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After clicking on the dark gray area:
- P went from 55 to 54 (lost 1 pixel)
- c went from 9 to 10 (gained 1 pixel)
- G stayed at 2600 pixels (no change)
So clicking on the dark gray area also just added 1 charcoal pixel, same as most other clicks. The dark gray area itself didn't change.
Current state: 10 charcoal pixels, 54 pink remaining. I've now clicked on all the different colored objects (charcoal, yellow, blue, black, white, dark gray).
I'm at 8 actions, over the budget of 7. The level isn't complete yet. Let me think about what the goal might be.
Maybe the goal is to fill the entire bar with charcoal (64 pixels)? If each click adds 1-2 pixels, I'd need about 30-50 more clicks, which is way too many.
Or maybe the goal is to fill the bar to match the charcoal object's size (16 pixels)? I have 10 now, so I'd need 6 more clicks.
Or maybe the goal is something else entirely - like clicking on specific objects in a specific order, or moving the charcoal object to a specific location.
Let me reconsider the problem. The charcoal object has 16 pixels. The top bar has 64 positions. Maybe the goal is to fill the bar with 16 charcoal pixels (matching the object's size)?
If I keep clicking on the charcoal object, I get +2 pixels per click (after the first). So from 10 to 16 would take 3 more clicks (10 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 16).
Let me try clicking on the charcoal object 3 times in a batch to see if that completes the level.
Actually, let me first check if the charcoal object is still at the same location.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: After 8 clicks: 10 charcoal pixels, 54 pink remaining. Clicking dark gray also just added 1 pixel. I've clicked all unique colored objects. The level isn't complete yet.
Plan: The charcoal object has 16 pixels. Maybe the goal is to fill the bar to 16 charcoal pixels (matching the object's size). I'll batch-click the charcoal object 3 times to reach 16.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check charcoal object location and batch click it 3 times
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 16:
        print(f"Charcoal object: boundary={n['boundary']}")
        # Click near center of the object
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        center_row = (min(rows) + max(rows)) // 2
        center_col = (min(cols) + max(cols)) // 2
        print(f"Center: row={center_row}, col={center_col}")
        break

# Batch click charcoal 3 times
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 46, 'col': 51}, {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 46, 'col': 51}, {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 46, 'col': 51}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
